origin.question: [µ¢ćõ╗Č 1: 1_s19_19.21.md]\n\nProblem 19.21. If an idealized $\mathbf{k} = 0$ matter dominated Friedmann cosmology with Hubble constant $\mathrm{H}_0$ contains homogeneously distributed sources of constant luminosity $\mathrm{L}$ , and if the local number density of such sources in space is now $\mathfrak{n}$ , what is the brightness $\mathbf{B}$ of the night sky (energy per steradian of sky per collection area per time)? (If the universe were static and infinitely old the brightness would be infinite; this is called Olbers' Paradox.)
origin.solution:
Solution 19.21. For a $\mathbf{k} = 0$ Friedmann universe we have
$$
\mathrm {R (t) / R_{0}} = \left(\frac {3}{2} \mathrm {H_{0} t}\right)^{\frac {2}{3}}.
$$
The energy emitted per source per unit proper time is L. This is redshifted by a factor $\mathbb{R}(\mathfrak{t}) / \mathbb{R}_0$ , so the total energy per star which is now
present is
$$
\tilde {\mathcal {E}} = \int_{0}^{t_{0}} L \frac {R (t)}{R_{0}} d t = \frac {3}{5} L \left(\frac {3}{2} H_{0}\right)^{\frac {2}{3}} t_{0}^{5 / 3}
$$
or $\mathfrak{S} = 2\mathrm{L} / 5\mathrm{H}_0$ since $t_0 = 2 / 3\mathrm{H}_0$ . The energy per volume, u, now is thus $ rac{2}{5}\mathrm{H}_0^{-1}\mathrm{Ln}$ . Since the energy fills the universe isotropically, it corresponds to a flux
$$
\mathrm {\bf B} = \frac {\mathrm {\bf c}}{4 \pi} \mathrm {\bf u} = \frac {\mathrm {\bf c}}{1 0 \pi} \mathrm {\bf H}_{0}^{- 1} \mathrm {\bf L n}.
$$
[By comparison, a Newtonian universe where the stars ŌĆ£ŌĆśturned onŌĆÖŌĆØ a time $t_0 = \left(\frac{3}{2} H_0\right)^{-1}$ ago would have
$$
\mathfrak {E} = \int_{0}^{t_{0}} L d t = \frac {2}{3} H_{0}^{- 1} L
$$
and
$$
\mathrm {\bf B} = \frac {\mathrm {c}}{6 \pi} \left. \mathrm {\bf H}_{0}^{- 1} \mathrm {\bf L n .}\right]
]$

cached_fact_sheet.kretschmann: \frac{2 \left(- r^{4} \left(r^{2} \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)} + r^{2} + 1\right) R^{4}{\left(t \right)} \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2} + r^{4} \left(r^{2} \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)} \left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{4} + r^{2} \left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{4} - \left(\frac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2}\right) \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)} + r^{2} \left(\left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{4} - \left(\frac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2}\right) \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)} + r^{2} \left(\left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{4} - \left(\frac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2}\right) + \left(r^{2} \left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2} - 1\right)^{2} \sin^{4}{\left(\theta \right)} + \left(r^{2} \left(\frac{d}{d t} R{\left(t \right)}\right)^{2} - 1\right)^{2}\right)}{r^{4} R^{4}{\left(t \right)} \sin^{2}{\left(\theta \right)}}
